FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study data (2016-2020), 6,749 adolescents aged 12-13, 21 recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; linear regression models estimating associations between ACE score and screen time/physical activity

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (self-reported hours per day across 7 modalities: texting, social media, video chatting, browsing, gaming, streaming) paired with individual-trait-level outcome: ACE score (trauma exposure). ACEs qualify as within-person construct.

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential analysis performed; adjusted linear regression models with numeric results

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported throughout: adjusted model B=0.44 (95% CI 0.26-0.62), p<.001 for ACE→screen time; multiple regression coefficients with significance tests and confidence intervals

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (May 2020 snapshot), not longitudinal; EC7 rule does not apply
notes: Strong paper; ACEs as exposure, screen time as outcome. Direction is clear and meets FT-IC2 criteria. No longitudinal follow-up so EC7 does not trigger.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD dataset, 9,855 children ages 9-10 at baseline with 2-year follow-up; U.S. cohort explicitly stated

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; longitudinal measurement invariance model and latent change score model examining screen time impact on intelligence change over 2 years

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal publication, Nature Scientific Reports 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time construct bundles three modalities (watching, socializing, gaming) without per-modality breakdown isolating phone or social media. Methods state 'we analyzed major screen time categories separately' but these categories are behavioral activities (watching, gaming, socializing), not device-specific modalities. This is multi-activity composite with no phone/SM isolation. Fails FT-IC2 requirement for qualifying SM/phone variable.

notes: Exclusion code FT-EC2. The paper measures generic screen time activities (watching, gaming, socializing) as composite without device-specific breakdown isolating phone or SM. Does not meet FT-IC2 conceptual requirement for qualifying SM/phone exposure.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD cohort, 11,000+ children aged 9-11; U.S. study with genetics, neuroimaging, behavioral data from multiple timepoints

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses including cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) and mediation models

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time utilization (STU) measured as total self-reported screen time (validated construct in ABCD); paired with ADHD trait (individual-trait-level behavioral/neuropsychiatric outcome). ADHD symptom severity qualifies as individual-trait-level variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential analyses performed: cross-sectional correlations (r values reported), longitudinal CLPM, mediation analyses with path coefficients

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results throughout: Pearson correlations (e.g., STU with FA values, p<.05), CLPM path coefficients (β=0.016, β=0.044), mediation indirect effects with bootstrap CIs. Multiple numeric results across main and supplementary analyses.

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional/cross-lagged design: STU and ADHD are both IV and DV in longitudinal analyses (CLPM structure). EC7 rule requires DV-only with no SM-as-IV anywhere; this paper has SM as both predictor and outcome. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Strong longitudinal paper. Clear evidence of bidirectional associations between STU and ADHD across cross-sectional, longitudinal, and mediation pathways. All criteria met.
